title: What recovery actually looks like on a Tuesday afternoon
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:

htmlContent:
Recovery doesn't happen in the big moments

We've all been sold this image. The dramatic breakthrough. The tearful catharsis. The moment where everything clicks and suddenly you're free. Right?

I believed in that fantasy for years. I kept waiting for my life to change in a single afternoon. Some kind of emotional explosion that would vaporize all the old patterns and leave me standing clean and new.

That's not how it works.

Here's the thing. Recovery happens on a Tuesday afternoon. It happens at 2:47 PM on a random day in October. It happens when nobody is watching and nothing special is happening and you're just trying to get through the next hour.

I remember one specific Tuesday. I was standing in my kitchen making a sandwich. Nothing dramatic. No trigger. No flashback. Just me, bread, turkey, and the sudden awareness that I wasn't bracing for anything. My shoulders weren't up by my ears. My jaw wasn't clenched. I wasn't scanning the room for threats. I was just making a sandwich.

And I started crying. Not from pain. From the sheer absurd relief of noticing that my body had stopped preparing for a disaster that wasn't coming.

This is what recovery actually looks like. It's not fireworks. It's noticing that you can breathe without effort. It's the moment you realize you haven't dissociated in three days. It's the quiet Tuesday when you catch yourself feeling okay and don't immediately panic that something must be wrong.

Peter Levine, the trauma researcher who developed Somatic Experiencing, would tell you this is exactly where healing lives. Not in the story you tell about what happened. Not in the dramatic reenactment. But in the tiny, almost invisible shifts in your nervous system. The moment when your body decides it can relax for one second. That second is everything.

The myth of the linear climb

We want recovery to be a line going up. We want each day to be better than the last. We want progress to feel like progress.

But your nervous system doesn't work that way. It never has. It never will.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades studying how trauma reshapes the brain and body. He found that recovery is not a straight path. It's a spiral. You circle back to the same places again and again, but each time you're a little different. Each time you see something new. Each time you have a little more capacity.

On that Tuesday afternoon when I made my sandwich, I had been in recovery for about four years. Four years of therapy. Four years of reading every book. Four years of feeling like I was failing because I still had bad days. Because I still got triggered. Because I still sometimes ended up curled on the bathroom floor at 3 AM.

But here's what I didn't see. The bathroom floor episodes had gone from three times a week to once every few months. The triggers that used to destroy me for days now only rattled me for an hour. The flashbacks had become less vivid, less frequent, less devastating.

I was so focused on the fact that I wasn't "cured" that I missed the fact that I was healing. Right in front of me. Every single day.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker wrote it specifically for people who grew up in ongoing trauma, not single-incident trauma. It's the kind of book you read in pieces, put down, cry, pick up again. It doesn't promise you a straight line. It promises you a path.

What Tuesday actually asks of you

Recovery on a Tuesday looks boring. That's the part nobody warns you about.

It's choosing to eat lunch when you don't feel hungry because your body forgot how to recognize its own needs. It's taking a shower even though the water pressure and temperature feel like too many decisions. It's sending one email when you wanted to send zero. It's leaving the house for five minutes when you wanted to stay inside for five days.

It's small. It's repetitive. It's undramatic.

And it's the only thing that actually works.

Janina Fisher, who specializes in trauma-informed treatment, talks about this constantly. She says healing happens in the gap between the old survival response and a new choice. That gap might last two seconds. But in those two seconds, you are rewiring your nervous system. You are telling your brain: "I am safe now. I don't need to fight, flee, or freeze. I can just be here."

Stay with me here. This is the part that matters most.

You don't heal by having one perfect day. You heal by having a thousand imperfect Tuesdays. By failing and trying again. By falling apart and putting yourself back together, slightly differently each time. By the boring, repetitive, unglamorous work of just being alive in your body for one more afternoon.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It names something that so many of us grew up with but couldn't articulate. The feeling that our emotions were too much. That our needs were an inconvenience. That we had to shrink ourselves to be acceptable. Reading it can feel like someone finally saw what you lived through.

The quiet revolution of the body

Here's what I wish someone had told me at the beginning. Recovery is not about thinking differently. It's about being differently in your body.

You can think all the right thoughts. You can understand your trauma intellectually. You can name every defense mechanism and attachment style and trigger pattern. And still, your body will be locked in the past. Still, your nervous system will be bracing for a blow that stopped landing years ago.

The work of recovery is the work of returning to your body. Over and over. Without judgment. Without forcing. Without demanding that it feel good.

On that Tuesday afternoon, I wasn't doing anything special. I wasn't meditating. I wasn't doing breathwork. I wasn't following a protocol. I was just standing in my kitchen, making a sandwich, and suddenly my body let go of something it had been holding for thirty years.

It didn't happen because I forced it. It happened because I had created enough safety, enough consistency, enough repetition of small safe moments, that my nervous system finally trusted it could relax for a second.

This is what Stephen Porges called the polyvagal theory. Your nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety or danger. When it finds enough cues of safety, it can shift out of defense mode and into connection mode. But those cues have to be real. They have to be consistent. They have to be repeated hundreds and thousands of times before your body starts to believe them.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Boundaries are one of the most concrete ways you can tell your nervous system "I am safe now." Every time you say no to something that drains you, you are creating a cue of safety. Every time you protect your time and energy, you are signaling to your body that you are no longer in survival mode.

The Tuesday that never ends

I wish I could tell you that after that Tuesday, everything was different. That I never dissociated again. That I never ended up on the bathroom floor. That I became some enlightened, healed person who floats through life untroubled.

That would be a lie.

That Tuesday was four years ago. I still have bad days. I still get triggered. I still sometimes forget that I'm allowed to have needs. I still catch myself bracing for disaster, scanning rooms, preparing for the worst.

But here's what changed. The bad days don't last as long. The triggers don't hit as hard. The forgetting doesn't last as long. And when I end up on the bathroom floor, I know I'll get up again. I've done it before. I'll do it again.

Recovery is not a destination. It's a direction. You don't arrive. You keep moving. Some days you move forward. Some days you move sideways. Some days you move backward. But you keep moving.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman wrote it decades ago, and it's still one of the most accurate descriptions of what this process actually looks like. She talks about the stages of recovery. Safety first. Then remembrance and mourning. Then reconnection. But she also acknowledges that these stages overlap and loop back on themselves. That recovery is not a ladder. It's a river.

So if you're reading this on a Tuesday afternoon, and you're tired, and you're wondering if you're doing it wrong, let me tell you something.

You're not doing it wrong.

The fact that you're still here, still trying, still the boring repetitive work of being alive, that's the whole thing. That's recovery. Right there. In this moment. On this Tuesday.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did your shoulders drop even a millimeter? Did your breath get even slightly easier? Did you feel even a flicker of recognition? That's it. That's the work. That's the Tuesday afternoon revolution.

Keep going. You're already there.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link)
